coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot with covalently linked Rhodamine dye: a high efficiency photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Dinda, Diptiman
Park, Hyunho
Lee, Hyun-Jun
Oh, Sangyoon
Park, Soo Young
coredata.dc:description: Designing metal free photocatalyst for solar water splitting is an emerging field of research. Although graphene quantum dot (GQD) has been explored as a potential photocatalyst, their performance remains inherently limited by poor visible light absorption and p-type conductivity. To address this problem, we have covalently functionalized GQD with visible light active photosensitizer Rhodamine 123. It was found that this dye-sensitized GQD could make quite stabilized photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) under visible light irradiation, which is in striking contrast to the virtually inactive photocatalytic HER performance of control GQD without dye. Efficient photo-induced electron transfer from Rhodamine 123 to GQD and also the transformation of p type GQD to the n-p ambipolar semiconductor, both boosted by covalent bonding, are responsible for such high photocatalytic HER performance. Observation of negative photocurrent at zero bias voltage during transient photocurrent measurement, also suggests its promising potential in the metal free photocathode system to achieve photoelectrochemical H2 production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot (GQD)
Covalent dye-sensitization
n-p ambipolar semiconductor
Metal free photocatalyst
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images along with particle size distribution (inset), (b) high resolution image showing lattice plane with d spacing of our synthesized Rho-GQD material. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Comparison of (a) FTIR spectra, (b) low resolution XPS spectra of as synthesized GQD and Rho-GQD. High resolution (c) C1S and (d) N1S XPS spectra and their deconvolution results of Rho-GQD. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Comparison of H2 evolution efficiency between covalently bonded dye-sensitized Rho-GQDs system and Rho/GQDs system attached via electrostatic interactions along with virgin GQDs and Rhodamine 123 dye; (b) photocatalytic HER efficiency of covalently bonded Rho-GQDs with different wt% of Rho after 4 h of visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation; (c) stability test under longer time exposure of visible light and (d) photocatalytic HER performance with Rho-GQDs in presence of 2 wt%-Pt as a co-catalyst during photocatalytic water splitting under visible light irradiation using 10 vol% TEOA as sacrificial electron donor. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Transient Photocurrent response of GQDs and Rho-GQDs under simulated solar light irradiation, (b) Mott-Schottky plot of Rho-GQDs; Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements: (c) Nyquist plots of Rho-GQDs and GQDs at zero voltage and (d) Nyquist plot of Rho-GQDs at different applied potentials in presence and absence of simulated solar light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Tauc plot for band gap calculation of (a) GQDs and (b) Rhodamine 123; (c) CB position of GQDs by cathodic linear sweep voltammetry measurements; (d) oxidation potential curve of Rhodamine 123 b y cyclic voltammetry measurements and (e) energy profile diagram of dye-sensitized Rho-GQDs system for H2 evolution under visible light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Change in lifetimes of Rho-GQDs after addition of sacrificial electron donor TEOA. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram of the covalent functionalization of GQD with Rhodamine 123 via amide bond forming coupling reaction resulting in Rho-GQD.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot with covalently linked Rhodamine dye: a high efficiency photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Supramolecular Optoelectronic Materials, Department of Material Science and Engineering, Seoul National University, 08826, Seoul, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Laboratory of Supramolecular Optoelectronic Materials, Department of Material Science and Engineering, Seoul National University, 08826, Seoul, South Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing metal free photocatalyst for solar water splitting is an emerging field of research. Although graphene quantum dot (GQD) has been explored as a potential photocatalyst, their performance remains inherently limited by poor visible light absorption and p-type conductivity. To address this problem, we have covalently functionalized GQD with visible light active photosensitizer Rhodamine 123. It was found that this dye-sensitized GQD could make quite stabilized photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) under visible light irradiation, which is in striking contrast to the virtually inactive photocatalytic HER performance of control GQD without dye. Efficient photo-induced electron transfer from Rhodamine 123 to GQD and also the transformation of p type GQD to the n-p ambipolar semiconductor, both boosted by covalent bonding, are responsible for such high photocatalytic HER performance. Observation of negative photocurrent at zero bias voltage during transient photocurrent measurement, also suggests its promising potential in the metal free photocathode system to achieve photoelectrochemical H2 production.

Image 1
Rhodamine123 attached Graphene quantum dot (GQD) acts as a metal free photocatalyst for H2 production under visible light irradiation. Covalent linkage facilitates better electron transfer as well as superior e-h separation by passivating charge trap centers in GQD making it ‘n-p’ type ambipolar semiconductor for efficient H2 evolution reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, searching for a renewable and clean energy is an important and urgent issue concerning global energy demand. Hydrogen (H2) is one of the most promising substituent to serve as an alternative fuel with the highest energy density of 140 MJ kg−1 [1]. Currently, H2 is mainly produced from different fossil fuels like natural gas, oil or coal, and only 2% via water splitting [2]. Since solar energy is the most powerful and richest source of renewable energy, H2 production by efficient combination of water and solar energy has enormous capacity to fulfill the present and future energy demand across the world in an eco-friendly manner [3]. After the first demonstration of photocatalytic water splitting by Fujishima and Honda in 1972 [4], notable progress has been made in last few decades to increase photocatalytic activity [5,6]. Mainly, different transition metal oxides and sulfides have been extensively explored aiming at the development of highly efficient photocatalysts [7–9]. Nevertheless, the disadvantages of low light absorption, rapid charge recombination, toxicity and instability of those transition metal catalysts demand searching for novel earth abundant and environmental friendly metal free photocatalyst for solar H2 production [10,11].

After its revolutionary discovery in 2004, graphene has extensively been investigated for different energy conversion applications all over the world [12]. Graphene, obtainable from earth abundant carbon based materials, consisting of a two-dimensional sp [2]-hybridized hexagonal carbon network, holds the strong potential to be an ideal photocatalyst for solar hydrogen production owing to its unique properties such as outstanding room-temperature mobility of charge carriers (2 × 105 cm2 V−1 s−1), high conductivity (106 S cm−1), large specific surface area (2630 m2 g−1) and excellent optical transmittance (∼ 97.7%) [13–15]. Unfortunately, however, the zero band gap nature of graphene intrinsically restricts its application as active catalyst in solar water splitting [16]. Aiming at the photocatalyst application, therefore, different strategies have been contrived for band-gap opening of graphene either by chemical modifications or by quantum confinement effect. In 2010, Yeh et al. have first demonstrated that the oxidized product of graphene, i.e. graphene oxide (GO), can produce H2 from methanol solution under UV blocked mercury lamp irradiation [17]. Subsequently, Garcia et al. showed that doping hetero atoms like phosphorous and nitrogen in the graphene surface is more effective for H2 production compared to GO [18,19]. Unfortunately, however, the photocatalytic HER rates of them remain below 15 μmol g−1 h−1 under visible light irradiation (given in Table S1) which is too small to be useful in practical applications [17–20].

On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), consisting of small graphene fragments sized between 1 and 20 nm stabilized with different carboxylic, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups at edge sites and basal planes, have also drawn increasing attention because of their unique physical and chemical properties compared to conventional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots [21,22]. Going from micrometer to nanometer size, band gap is opened up for GQDs due to quantum confinement effect [23]. With appropriate size and surface modifications, GQDs have been demonstrated to be useful in different applications like photocatalysis, optical sensing, bio-imaging, light-emitting diode, and photovoltaics [24–29]. In 2014, Teng et al. have successfully demonstrated that nitrogen doped graphene oxide quantum dots (NGO-QDs) could be used for overall water splitting to give H2 and O2 in molar ratio of 2:1 at the rate of 0.5 and 0.25 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively, under visible light irradiation [30]. Although there have been a few subsequent research works with other GQDs, their efficiency in terms of photocatalytic HER rate was 53 μmol g−1 h−1 at most even under UV-unfiltered light irradiation (see Table S1) [31–34]. Under visible light irradiation, however, ammonia-treated nitrogen-doped graphene oxide dots (A-NGODs) showed the highest ever photocatalytic HER rate of 37 μmol g−1 h−1 [33]. As for such small H2 evolution rate of GQDs, we came to note their low absorbance in visible light region. Dye sensitization has been proven to be an effective method to enhance the light absorption of catalyst in visible region. Although, dye sensitization has been extensively studied in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [35–37], the photocatalytic HER of dye-sensitized semiconductor has rarely been explored. In 2011, Min et al. have used Eosin Y sensitized reduced graphene oxide (RGO) sheets with Pt co-catalyst for H2 production under visible light irradiation [38]. Among others reported so far, highest photocatalytic HER rate of 250 μmol g−1 h−1 was reported by RGO with covalently attached ruthenium dye when combined with 5 wt% Pt co-catalyst under visible light irradiation [40]. Whilst several dye-sensitized systems of RGO sheets were reported [38–40] (given in Table S1), we could find no report on the dye-sensitized system of GQDs targeted for photocatalytic application.

In this work, we decided to covalently attach Rhodamine 123 (Rho) dye as a photosensitizer to GQD to make the system active for photocatalytic HER under visible light irradiation. Selection of Rhodamine 123 was rationalized because its lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy was higher than the conduction band edge of GQD (vide infra) to enable the photo-induced electron transfer from the former to the latter. We have first synthesized GQD by cutting the size of GO with concurrent reduction via hydrothermal treatment. Then, the amide coupling reaction between the primary amine group of Rhodamine 123 and the carboxylic group of GQD was made to transform p type GQD to n-p ambipolar type semiconductor giving the covalently dye-sensitized system of Rho-GQDs as depicted in Scheme 1 . This type of covalent attachment not only helps to transfer of photoinduced electrons rapidly from LUMO of dye to conduction band of GQD, also restore n-type conductivity by passivated charge trap centers in GQD making it efficient catalyst for hydrogen production under visible light. We have also done different photoelectrochemical measurements with transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopic analysis to confirm this mechanism in Rho-GQD under visible light.

Graphite powder was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Rhodamine 123 was purchased from ACROS Organics. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 wt%), hydrogen chloride (HCl, 35%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), potassium per manganate (KMnO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Merck Chemicals. N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) from Sigma-Aldrich and 4-methyl morpholine (NMM) from Alfa Aesar were purchased. All the chemicals were of analytical-grade and used without any further purification.

In the first step, we have synthesized GO from graphite powder with some modifications in the well-known Hummers method [41]. The details of the synthesis procedure is given in the supporting information.

In the second step, we have used as-synthesized GO for the preparation of GQD. For this, we have taken 10 mL (3.5 mg/mL) of GO solution in a beaker and diluted it to 30 mL by adding distilled water. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h for homogeneous dispersion. Then, the pH of mixture was adjusted to 8 by adding dropwise 2 N NaOH solution followed by further sonication for 1 h. Finally, this black solution was transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave and was treated at 200 °C for 16 h. After cooling to room temperature, the filtrate solution was collected using 0.2 μm Millipore membrane filter and was dialyzed by 1 KDa dialysis kit. We have used this GQD solution for further reactions.

In the final step, we have covalently attached Rhodamine 123 to the carboxylic groups of the as synthesized GQD via amide bond formation reaction to make Rho-GQDs as shown in Scheme 1. For this, 10 mL (0.5 mg/mL) of as synthesized GQDs solution was diluted to 50 mL with distilled water. Then, 40 μL of NMM was added into it and the resulting mixture solution was stirred for 30 min. 50 mg of EDC and 25 mg of NHS were sequentially added and the resulting solution was stirred in dark. After 1 h, 10 mg of Rhodamine 123 was added into that solution with stirring. Then, the reaction mixture was stirred for 24 h under dark for amide bond formation. We have collected bright yellowish colored solution after filtering the reaction mixture with Millipore membrane followed by dialyzing with 1 KDa dialysis kit to remove unreacted reactants as well as Rhodamine. Finally, we could collect covalently attached Rho-GQD, which was preserved in dark before using it as photocatalysts for solar water splitting.

We have synthesized different batches of Rho-GQDs by adding different amounts of Rhodamine 123 (2.5, 5, 10, 20 and 30 mg) into 10 mL of GQD solution to examine the effect of attached dye amount to the photocatalytic activity. Actual amount of attached Rhodamine dyes on GQD could be calculated by using the calibration curve of Rhodamine dye absorption at 500 nm through UV–Vis absorption experiments as shown in Fig. S1.

To investigate the covalent bonding effect which should be different from the ionic association effect between Rhodamine 123 and GQD, simple ionically attached system denoted as Rho/GQD was also prepared following the same procedure without addition of EDC-NHS.

We have characterized our synthesized GO, GQD and Rho-GQD by using different spectroscopic as well as microstructural analysis. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were made by Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and AXIS-HSi (KRATOS) systems, respectively. Powder X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD) data were obtained by using Bruker D8 Advance with a diffractometer equipped with CuKα (λ = 1.5418 Å) radiation. Raman analysis was done by DXR 2 Raman Microscope of Thermo Fisher Scientific. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed using a PSIA instrument XE-150 and a Bruker instrument multimode with a Nano Scope V controller. JEOL-JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was used for morphological and elemental analysis. UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence measurements were carried out by using Shimadzu UV-1650 PC and Carry Varion spectrophotometer, respectively. Fluo Time 200 spectrometer (Pico Quant) equipped with a Pico Harp300 TCSPC board and a PMA182 photomultiplier was used for fluorescence life time measurements.

We have done all the photocatalytic HER measurements in a gas enclosed Pyrex vial system. To a fixed amount of the Rho-GQD solution, 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) was added as sacrificial reagent. Total volume was maintained to 13 mL after adjusting the pH of the solution to 7 with 2 N HCl and NaOH solutions. After gas purging for 15 min by Ar mixed with 1% CH4, the sample vial was irradiated with 300 W xenon lamp using 400 nm cut-off filters to eliminate the UV light. We have calculated the amount of evolved H2 gas by Agilent 7890A gas chromatography (equipped with thermal conductivity detector and 5 Å molecular sieve column) during the progress of the photocatalytic measurements.

Furthermore, to check the photocatalytic HER efficiency of the Rho-GQDs in the presence of co-catalyst, photocatalytic reactions have been carried out with 2 wt% Pt co-catalyst. For this, we have taken 0.285 mL of 1 mM H2PtCl6·6H2O (99.9%; Alfa Aesar, USA) salt solution and mixed it with the 10 ml (0.28 mg/mL) of Rho-GQD solution followed by addition of 10 vol% TEOA and maintained the volume to 13 ml after adjusting pH to 7. After degassing for 15 min we have irradiated the mixture with 300 W xenon lamp using 400 nm cut-off filters to form colloidal Pt nano particles in-situ in it via photo deposition process during the H2 production reaction.

All the electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out by Vertex.One (Ivium technology) electrochemical analyzer using standard three electrode system with 0.1 M KPF6 aqueous solution as the supporting electrolyte. Here, we have used Rho-GQD or GQD materials as the working electrode by drop casting them over ITO coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plates. Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. Oxidation potential of Rhodamine 123 was measured by cyclic voltammetry using 0.01 M AgNO3/Ag as a reference electrode, glassy carbon disc (diameter = 3 mm) as a working electrode and Pt wire as a counter electrode. The redox potential of reference electrode was calibrated using ferrocenium/ferrocene (Fc+/Fc) as an internal standard. Here, 0.1 M NBu4PF6 in anhydrous acetonitrile was used as the supporting electrolyte. Solar simulator model PEC L01 with 100 mW cm−2 irradiance was used as the light source for photoelectrochemical measurements.

First, we have characterized our synthesized GO and GQDs via different microstructural and spectroscopic analysis like AFM, XRD, XPS and Raman to confirm the formation of few nanometer sized GQDs from micrometer sized GO sheets after hydrothermal treatment. We have given all the detailed descriptions of them in supporting information as shown in Figs. S2–S5.

Then, we have characterized as synthesized covalently attached Rho-GQD material. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of Rho-GQD in Fig. 1 (a) shows that it has quite homogeneous distribution in particle size between 3 and 5 nm: note however that the actual size must be somewhat larger because the Rhodamine corona is not seen in this TEM image. The observed lattice spacing of Rho-GQD is 0.24 nm, correlated with (1120) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene as shown in Fig. 1(b). The average particle size and lattice spacing are quite similar to those of virgin GQD (shown in Fig. S6(a and b)), suggesting no change in physical structure in the core GQD structure after covalent attachment of Rhodamine dye.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the FTIR spectra of as synthesized GQD and Rho-GQD. GQD shows different characteristic peaks at 3440, 1086, and 1600 cm−1 for stretching vibration of O–H, C–O, and CC bonds, respectively [42]. The peaks at 1430 and 605 cm−1 correspond to C–OH stretching and C–H bending vibrations, respectively. We can also see the main characteristic peaks of Rhodamine at 3300 and 3100, 1710, 1590, 1470, 1265 and 762 cm−1 for N–H stretching, CO stretching of ester, CC stretching of benzene rings and NH2 stretching, C–O–C stretching of pyran ring and NH2 bending respectively. After attachment to GQD, all these peaks are present in Rho-GQD composite along with several new peaks. A new peak at 1630 cm−1 is related with amide bond formation between carboxylic groups of GQD and one amine group of Rhodamine [43]. Other peaks at 1550 and 1326 cm−1 corresponding to N–H in plane and C–N stretching vibrations of amide bond have arisen due to covalent attachment [44]. Apart from these, strong peak at 2970 cm−1 in Rho-GQD is also attributed to the C–H stretching of methyl ester group of Rhodamine 123. All these data clearly confirm covalent attachment of Rhodamine 123 to GQD. Besides these, peaks at 3330 and 1470 cm−1 are attributed to the N–H stretching vibrations of one remaining free amine group of Rhodamine in Rho-GQD.

From the low resolution XPS curve in Fig. 2(b), we can observe that GQD shows typical peaks at 284 and 540 eV for carbon and oxygen, respectively, whereas Rho-GQD shows one additional strong peak at ∼ 400 eV for nitrogen. We have also deconvoluted the C1S spectra to analyze the functional moieties present in it. After deconvolution, C1S spectrum of GQD shows typical C–C peak along with C–O, CO, and HO–CO peaks as shown in Fig. S4(b). On the other hand, C1S spectra of Rho-GQD shows additional C–N peak along with other four peaks of GQD as shown in Fig. 2(c) after deconvolution. Here, the presence of new C–N peak along with reduced intensity of C–O/CO peak clearly reveals that Rhodamine is covalently attached to GQD. Furthermore, we have deconvoluted N1S spectrum of Rhodamine only and Rho-GQD samples to confirm the formation of covalent linkage. The deconvoluted XPS data of Rhodamine in Fig. S7 shows the presence of only free amine peak (C–NH-) in it. Whereas, for Rho-GQD, there is an additional strong peak at ∼401.5 eV for amide (–HN–CO) bond as shown in Fig. 2(d) along with amine peak. This peak only arises due to the covalent attachment of amines of Rhodamine to the carboxylic groups of GQD via amide linkage resulting in the synthesis of Rho-GQDs [45,46].

Rho-GQD was evaluated as the photocatalyst for HER under visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation using 300 W Xenon lamp. We have done all the measurements using 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) as a sacrificial electron donor while maintaining pH of the solution to 7. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), Rho-GQD (with 64 wt % of Rhodamine) shows quite promising enhancement in H2 evolution. It shows initial photocatalytic HER rate of 488 μmol g−1 h−1 and can produce 940 μmol g−1 of H2 after 4 h without using any co-catalyst under visible light irradiation. We have also performed photocatalytic HER measurements with only GQD and Rhodamine separately. Neither GQD nor Rhodamine showed any photocatalytic HER activity under visible light irradiation. Therefore, Rhodamine molecules in Rho-GQD appear to act as photon antennae transferring excited state electrons to the GQD core very effectively boosted by the short covalently bonding. To compare, we have also measured photocatalytic HER activity of GQD complexed with Rhodamine (Rho/GQDs) through electrostatic interactions as shown in Fig. 3(a). It turned out that the covalently bonded Rho-GQD system shows 22 times faster H2 evolution rate compared to the ionically attached system, the latter showing highest photocatalytic HER rate only of 22 μmol g−1 h−1. The faster transfer of excited electrons from the photosensitizer to active catalyst and suppressed recombination in the covalently bonded case (vide infra) should be responsible for such large enhancement in photocatalytic HER activity compared to the electrostatically attached system [47]. To investigate the effect of attached amount of Rhodamine in Rho-GQD, we have measured photocatalytic HER activity of the samples with different weight ratio of Rhodamine to GQD.

As shown in Fig. 3(b), the photocatalytic HER activity gradually increased with increasing Rhodamine content and then decreased after showing maximum performance at 64 wt % of Rhodamine to GQD. At the higher Rhodamine content, photocatalytic HER activity is considered to decrease owing to the self-quenching of the excited dye molecules via non-radiative transitions. With the increase in dye amount, the distance between two dye molecules will decrease and there will be unfavorable exciton coupling (mostly H-type aggregation) or dipolar energy transfer between two adjacent dye molecules leading to quenching of excited states via promoted non-radiative deactivation pathway. So, the effective utilization of visible light will be decreased that eventually reduce the efficiency of photocatalytic HER [48–51]. Beside this, large amount of Rhodamine molecules surrounding the GQD core may block the access of H+ to the active sites of GQD resulting in the reduced H2 evolution efficiency. Therefore, there should be a compromise to give maximum photocatalytic HER in the degree of functionalization.

To test the photostability of Rho-GQD, we have done photocatalytic HER experiment for the extended period under visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation. From Fig. 3(c), we can see that after some initial decrease in activity, it shows quite steady H2 production even after 12 h of visible light irradiation and beyond. It produces 1360 μmol g−1 of H2 for 12 h while keeping their H2 evolution activity. We further checked its repeatability by measuring photocatalytic HER activity with 3 batches of Rho-GQD samples as shown in Fig. S8. Synthesized Rho-GQDs show quite similar photocatalytic HER activity in all the batches. Generally, dye-sensitized materials show quite poor stability under visible light irradiation. To further confirm the photostability of Rho-GQD, we have done XRD and XPS analysis of the sample after 12 h of photocatalytic HER experiment. From Fig. S9, no noticeable change in XRD profile of Rho-GQD is observed after photocatalytic HER. From the low resolution XPS spectra in Fig. S10(a), presence of prominent N1S peak also confirms better stability of Rho-GQD material compared to other common dye-sensitized materials that show poor photostability. Here, peak of N1S and O1S are slightly decreased compared to C1S also with small decrease in C–N peak intensity in the deconvoluted C1S spectra as shown in Fig. S10(b). It suggests small portion of the attached Rhodamine molecules is decomposed during long 12 h exposure of visible light. This result confirms that our synthesized covalently bonded dye sensitized system is quite stable for H2 production under visible light irradiation.

To evaluate the photocatalytic HER activity of Rho-GQD in the presence of additional co-catalyst, we have further conducted photocatalytic experiment of Rho-GQD with colloidal Pt. A large improvement in photocatalytic activity was observed by using 2 wt% Pt as the co-catalyst as shown in Fig. 3(d). It can produce H2 steadily at a constant rate of 1.4 mmol g−1 h−1 even after 12 h of visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation. Presumably, the presence of Pt colloids further restricts the backward recombination reaction in Rho-GQD by fast draining of transferred electron to Pt to give enhanced H2 evolution activity and photostability even after longer exposure to visible light [52,53]. We have also done TEM measurements with Pt loaded Rho-GQD sample. From the HR-TEM and STEM images in Fig. S11 (a and b), we can see that colloidal Pt nano particles (bright spots in STEM image) are homogeneously mixed with our Rhodamine attached GQDs (hazy spots in STEM image) sample by in situ photo deposition during HER. Furthermore, we have also checked photocatalytic HER performance of only GQD sample in presence of Pt co-catalyst. GQD cannot produce H2 even with Pt catalyst under visible light as shown in Fig. 3(d). Thus, our dye sensitized Rho-GQD system is quite efficient to produce H2 with or without using any noble metal catalyst under visible light irradiation compared to previously reported results of GQDs. We have summarized and compared their photocatalytic HER activity with Rho-GQD in Table S1.

We have also measured different photoelectrochemical experiments with Rho-GQD regarding its superior photocatalytic activity. Transient photocurrent measurements were carried out for Rho-GQDs films by conventional three electrode system using 0.1 M KPF6 as the supporting electrolyte under simulated solar light illumination with UV cut-off filter. A prominent change in photocurrent is observed at zero bias voltage when we turn on the light as shown in Fig. 4 (a). This type of prominent photocurrent response is certainly due to the photoinduced electron transfer from the excited Rhodamine corona to GQD core, which is very stable and reproducible for the consecutive on-off repeating cycles under visible light irradiation at zero bias voltage. On the other hand, non-sensitized GQD does not show any noticeable changes in current after visible light irradiation on it, which is in accordance with its negligible photocatalytic HER activity mentioned above. This type of quick photo response in Rho-GQD implies the highly efficient electron-hole separation and decreased back electron recombination in Rho-GQD under visible light irradiation. To be further convinced of this theme, we have measured change in capacitance (C) with applied potential by electrochemical impedance analyzer, from which we have drawn Mott-Schottky (M-S) plot showing 1/C2 vs. applied potential. From Fig. 4(b), we find both n- and p-type semiconductor behaviors in Rho-GQDs. On the other hand, only p-type semiconductor behavior is observed in the M-S plot of as synthesized GQDs as shown in Fig. S12. It is most likely that the absence of n-type mobility in GQD is due to the electron trapping in the edge functional groups like carboxylic and hydroxyl moiety. On the other hand, these electron trapping sites are effectively passivated by covalent amide bond formation in Rho-GQD to restore n-type conductivity. Since electron mobility is essential for H2 production, the excellent photocatalytic HER activity of Rho-GQD and negligible activity of GQD are thus well rationalized. The presence of both n- and p-type semiconductor behaviors should invoke superior charge separation in Rho-GQDs during photocatalytic measurements [54]. Thus the covalent dye attachment in Rho-GQD facilitates the electron transfer from Rhodamine to GQD which eventually helps to separate electron-hole pair during photocatalytic reaction under visible light irradiation.

To better understand this charge separation process, the charge transfer resistance to Rho-GQD was measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis. The Nyquist plot in Fig. 4(c) shows much smaller semicircle radius for Rho-GQD compared to virgin GQD at open circuit voltage. It is known that charge transfer rate is inversely proportional to the radius of semicircle in Nyquist plot. Therefore, it implies that covalent attachment of Rhodamine dye to GQD shortens the ion diffusion pathways facilitating better charge transfer process across the electrode-electrolyte junction in Rho-GQD [55]. This eventually helps to exhibit such enhanced photocurrent efficiency along with better photocatalytic HER activity in Rho-GQD compared to GQD.

We have also measured the change in charge transfer resistance of Rho-GQD material at different applied voltages by EIS analysis under dark and visible light illumination. From the Nyquist plot in Fig. 4(d), we can clearly observe that Rho-GQD shows comparatively lower charge transfer resistance under visible light compared to that in dark at open circuit voltage condition, consistent with the photoinduced electron current in Rho-GQD shown in Fig. 4(a). With increase in external voltage, the charge transfer resistance is drastically decreased making the material more conducting and the change become more prominent in case of visible light irradiation making it more suitable for solar water splitting. All these results endorse the better electron-hole separation in Rho-GQDs under visible light that ultimately helps to drive HER reducing water proficiently [56].

To understand such superior catalytic activity of this dye sensitized Rho-GQD system, we have tried to analyze its energy diagram. At first, we have measured the UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQD, Rhodamine dye and Rho-GQD separately as shown in Fig. S13(a, b and c). By plotting the square of absorption energy (αE, where α is the absorbance) vs. photon energy (E) diagram following Tauc’s equation, direct energy gap could be calculated [57]. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), from the linear extrapolation in Tauc plot, we get quite wide band gap of 3.1 eV for GQD. This large band gap is attributed to the small size (3–5 nm) of GQD that is sufficient to overcome the thermodynamic energy requirement of 1.23 eV for water splitting. On the other hand, Rhodamine 123 shows comparatively narrow band gap of 2.35 eV as shown in Fig. 5(b).

The apparent energy levels of these materials could be determined by different electrochemical measurements. We have done linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements with cathodic potential scan and determined conduction band (CB) position of GQD from the intercept of linear extrapolation. The CB position of GQD was found to be −1.0 V (vs Ag/AgCl) as shown in Fig. 5(c) which is well above H2/H2O redox couple. Then, oxidation potential of Rhodamine was obtained by cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements as shown in Fig. 5(d) and Fig. S14. The calculated highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) positions of Rhodamine are −5.4 and −3.15 eV (vs. vacuum), respectively, where the latter was evaluated from the former and the optical band gap of 2.35 eV [58]. With all these data, we have drawn an energy band diagram for this dye sensitized Rho-GQD system regarding its H2 production under visible light irradiation as shown in Fig. 5(e). After visible light absorption by Rhodamine, the excited electrons can be quickly transferred from the LUMO of Rhodamine to the CB of GQD to catalyze water for H2 evolution. Here, holes are transferred to TEOA which is acting as a sacrificial electron donor during this photocatalysis process. The reaction process is operated via reductive quenching mechanism as shown in Fig. S15. To confirm this, we have measured the fluorescence lifetime of only Rhodamine123, Rho-GQD and change in its lifetime after addition of TEOA at 530 nm emission wavelengths. All the lifetime data are presented in Table S2. From Fig. S16 and Table S2, we can observe that the lifetime of Rhodamine is not decreased after covalent attachment with GQD. Whereas, the lifetime of Rho-GQD is decreased gradually after addition of TEOA as shown in Fig. 6 and Table S2. The fluorescence intensity of Rho-GQD is also quenched by addition of TEOA as shown in Fig. S17. Therefore, after visible light irradiation, Rhodamine dye will first be excited and TEOA molecules will donate electrons to the excited Rhodamine molecule making it to radical anion (Rho∗¯) followed by scavenging holes to them. Then, those excited radical anions will transfer electrons to the active catalyst GQD for the reduction of water to H2 as shown in Fig. S15 [59,60].

In summary, we have successfully synthesized 3–5 nm sized dye-sensitized GQD by covalently attaching Rhodamine 123 to the GQD core. This Rho-GQD showed quite promising photocatalytic HER efficiency as metal free photocatalyst with initial rate of 488 μmol g−1 h−1 to produce 1360 μmol g−1 of H2 in 12 h and much more beyond under visible light irradiation. This is in striking contrast to the negligible photocatalytic HER performance of virgin GQD, signifying the role of covalent functionalization of Rhodamine 123. Through the electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements, it was found that the covalent functionalization of Rhodamine 123 not only secured the efficient photoinduced electron transfer to the GQD but also passivated the electron trap sites of GQD to restore the n-type semiconductivity of GQD resulting in the surprisingly high photocatalytic HER performance of Rho-GQD. Finally, observation of negative photocurrent response at zero bias voltage during transient photocurrent measurement suggests a promising potential towards designing of metal free photocathode materials for photoelectrochemical H2 production.

Diptiman Dinda: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Hyunho Park: Software, Resources, Validation. Hyun-Jun Lee: Project administration, Formal analysis. Sangyoon Oh: Formal analysis. Soo Young Park: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work at Seoul National University was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the NRF funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2017R1E1A1A01075372[RIAM0417-20200051]). D. Dinda acknowledges Brain Korea 21plus programme for fellowship support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Supporting information contains synthesis of GO, calibration curve to measure Rho wt%, Characterizations of GO and GQDs by AFM, XRD, XPS and Raman analysis, TEM and elemental mapping of GQDs, repeatability photocatalytic HER test, stability test after photocatalytic HER experiment, TEM of Pt-decorated Rho-GQD sample, UV–Vis absorption of GQD, Rhodamine 123 and Rho-GQD, Mott-Schottky plot of GQD, CV measurement of ferrocene, reductive quenching mechanism scheme, PL quenching of Rho-GQD along with corresponding TCSPC results and comparison between photocatalytic HER activities of different graphene based photocatalysts.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.06.041.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Diptiman Dinda: Obtained his Ph D degree on Chemistry from Dept. of Materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of science, India in December, 2017. He joined LSOM Lab, SNU as a postdoc fellow from March, 2018. His research interests include the designing of 2D heterostructures, semiconductor Quantum dots based on Graphene and transition metal chalcogenides along with their applications in optoelectronic and energy conversion fields. Currently, his research focus on the development of GQD based metal free photocatalysts for solar H2 production. Google scholar link is as follows. https://scholar.google.co.in/citations?user=R3E-c94AAAAJ&hl=en

Soo Young Park: Earned his PhD degree from Seoul National University in 1988. He has been a senior researcher in the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST, Korea) in 1985–1995. He joined Seoul National University as a faculty member in 1995 and was promoted and tenured to the full professor of Materials Science and Engineering in 2004. He has been a director of the Organic Nanophotonics National Research Laboratory (NRL) of Korea in 2004–2009 and a director of the Creative Research Initiative Center for Supramolcular Optoelectronics Materials (CSOM) in 2009–2018. He has been awarded with Shinyang Engineering Award (2005), Samsung Polymer Award (2007), Academic Research Award of Seoul National University (2009), and Sudang Prize (2016). In 2010, he was appointed as the fellow of Korean Academy of Science and Technology (KAST). His research areas include design and synthesis of molecular electronics and photonics materials related to the fluorescence, phosphorescence, electroluminescence, solar cell, transistor, battery, artificial photosynthesis, photochromism, sensing, optical recording, and nonlinear optics. Web of Science Researcher ID of Soo Young Park is G-1080-2012 and relevant Publons page is as follows. https://publons.com/researcher/2719016/soo-young-park/publications/
